name: Coffee

book_claims.claim: 3-4 cups per day achieves largest risk reduction for cardiovascular and cancer outcomes
book_claims.mechanism: Not fully specified in this chapter; epidemiological association

supplementary_research.finding: Umbrella review of 201 meta-analyses found coffee consumption consistently associated with reduced risk of all-cause mortality, cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and several cancers. Largest benefit at 3-4 cups/day with 17% lower all-cause mortality compared to non-drinkers.

Meta-analysis of 36 studies with over 1.2 million participants found a J-shaped relationship between coffee and cardiovascular mortality, with maximum benefit at 3-5 cups/day (significant 15% risk reduction). Both caffeinated and decaf showed benefits.

UK Biobank study (>170,000 participants, median 7-year follow-up) found 2-3 cups of ground, instant, or decaffeinated coffee per day associated with significant reductions in cardiovascular disease incidence and mortality. Ground coffee showed strongest association.

Strong evidence for reduced all-cause mortality, type 2 diabetes risk, and liver disease risk. Chlorogenic acids and caffeic acid identified as key bioactive compounds beyond caffeine. Filter coffee preferred over unfiltered (boiled/French press) due to diterpene removal.

culinary_pairings.source: Mocha; shared chlorogenic acid and flavanol compounds
Traditional Middle Eastern coffee spicing; complementary blood glucose effects
Breakfast pairing; but consume coffee between meals for iron absorption
Arabic/Turkish coffee tradition; adds complexity and digestive benefits

seasonality: Available year-round. Beans from different origins have seasonal harvests (October-March for Central America, April-August for East Africa). Freshly roasted and ground beans retain volatile compounds and chlorogenic acids better than pre-ground.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: 35-175 mg per cup (varies by roast and brewing method)
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Primary bioactive polyphenol. Light roasts retain more than dark roasts. Hydrolysed in the gut to caffeic acid, which is well absorbed.
Rapidly and nearly completely absorbed. Half-life 3-7 hours, varies by CYP1A2 genotype. Fast metabolisers may benefit more from coffee consumption.
Contributes to coffee's flavour and aroma. Demonstrates anti-diabetic and neuroprotective effects in animal models.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: Varies by preparation (high in unfiltered; removed by paper filter)
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Raise LDL cholesterol. Paper-filtered coffee removes 80-90% of diterpenes. This is why the book specifies filter coffee.

synergies.description: Shared chlorogenic acid family compounds; complementary flavanol profiles

warnings: Consume between meals, not with iron-rich foods -- polyphenols chelate iron and reduce absorption (see tea-coffee-cocoa entry)
Filter coffee preferred over French press/boiled to remove cholesterol-raising diterpenes
Avoid in late afternoon/evening due to caffeine half-life; can disrupt sleep quality
